diagrams.title: SMART Criteria Framework
diagrams.description: A comprehensive diagram showing the five SMART criteria as interconnected components forming a unified objective-setting framework
elements: Central hexagon labeled 'Project Objective'
Five surrounding pentagons connected to center: S-Specific (blue), M-Measurable (green), A-Achievable (orange), R-Relevant (purple), T-Time-bound (red)
Arrow from each pentagon pointing inward showing contribution to the objective
Each pentagon contains: definition, key questions, and example for that criterion
Outer ring showing relationships between criteria (e.g., Specific + Measurable = Clear success criteria)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to apply each SMART criterion systematically when writing project objectives, understanding how all five elements work together to create well-defined objectives

diagrams.title: Objective Hierarchy Pyramid
diagrams.description: A hierarchical pyramid structure showing the progression from high-level vision to actionable tasks
elements: Top tier (smallest): Vision/Strategy - labeled 'Organization's Long-term Vision'
Second tier: Business Goals - 3 boxes showing 'Increase Revenue', 'Improve Customer Satisfaction', 'Market Expansion'
Third tier: Project Objectives - derived from goals, showing 'Reduce system response time by 40%', 'Achieve 95% customer satisfaction', 'Launch in 3 new markets'
Fourth tier: Deliverables/Outcomes - concrete outputs like 'New mobile app', 'API integration', 'Training documentation'
Base tier (largest): Tasks/Activities - daily development tasks and actions
Dotted lines connecting upper tiers to lower tiers showing derivation
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how abstract business vision translates into concrete, actionable project objectives, and how each level of the hierarchy informs the next

diagrams.title: Objective Setting Process Flow
diagrams.description: A step-by-step flowchart showing the systematic process of setting project objectives
elements: Start node: 'Identify Stakeholders' rectangle with rounded corners
Decision diamond: 'Define Business Case?' - Yes/No paths
Process box: 'Conduct Market Analysis/Gather Requirements'
Process box: 'Identify Success Metrics'
Process box: 'Draft Objectives using SMART'
Decision diamond: 'Validate against Stakeholder Expectations?' with Yes/No
Process box: 'Refine and Prioritize Objectives'
Process box: 'Document in Project Charter'
End node: 'Objectives Approved'
Arrows showing sequential flow with decision points highlighted in yellow
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the systematic methodology for setting objectives, understanding the iterative nature of the process and the importance of stakeholder validation at each stage

diagrams.title: Stakeholder Alignment Matrix for Objectives
diagrams.description: A matrix diagram showing how different stakeholders contribute to and benefit from project objectives
elements: Table with rows: Client, Project Manager, Development Team, End Users, Quality Assurance
Columns: 'Objective Input', 'Key Interest', 'Success Metric', 'Communication Frequency'
Sample entries: Client input 'ROI > 20%', Interest 'On-time delivery', Metric 'Budget adherence'
Color coding: Green = High influence, Yellow = Medium, Blue = Low
Arrows showing information flow between stakeholders and objectives
Legend explaining color codes
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the importance of aligning objectives with diverse stakeholder needs and how different stakeholders contribute to objective definition

diagrams.title: Good vs Bad Objectives Comparison Block Diagram
diagrams.description: Side-by-side block diagram contrasting poorly-defined and well-defined objectives with transformation arrows
elements: Left block: 'Poorly-Defined Objectives' with examples: 'Improve performance', 'Make it user-friendly', 'Reduce costs'
Transformation arrow labeled 'Apply SMART Criteria' with gear icon
Right block: 'Well-Defined Objectives' showing: 'Reduce API response time from 2s to <500ms', 'Achieve System Usability Scale score >80', 'Reduce infrastructure costs by 25% within Q2'
Bottom section: Consequences - Poor = Scope creep, Missed deadlines, Budget overrun / Well-defined = Clear progress tracking, Stakeholder satisfaction, Project success
Color coding: Red tones for poor, Green tones for well-defined
diagrams.purpose: Students develop ability to distinguish between vague and precise objectives, reinforcing the practical application of SMART criteria through concrete examples

charts.title: SMART vs CLEAR Criteria Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table with columns: Criterion, SMART Definition, CLEAR Definition, Software Project Example. Rows: Collaborative (C), Limited (L), Emotional (E), Appreciable (A), Refinable (R) vs Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Shows that SMART is structured/goals-oriented while CLEAR is more adaptive/team-focused. Example: SMART 'Complete module by Dec 2024' vs CLEAR 'Team feels confident about module completion timeline'.

charts.title: Objective Validation Decision Flow
charts.content: Flowchart showing validation process: Start → Check Specific? → Check Measurable? → Check Achievable? → Check Relevant? → Check Time-bound? → All Yes = Approve → Any No = Revise → Loop back. Each decision diamond shows consequences: if No, specific feedback on what to fix. End states: Approved Objectives or Return for Revision with comments.

charts.title: Categories of Project Objectives
charts.content: Classification into four quadrants: (1) Technical Objectives - performance, quality, functionality; (2) Business Objectives - ROI, market share, cost reduction; (3) Strategic Objectives - competitive advantage, innovation, compliance; (4) Operational Objectives - efficiency, productivity, customer satisfaction. Each quadrant contains 3 example objectives relevant to software projects.

charts.title: Objective vs Goal vs Key Result
charts.content: Three-column comparison: Definition, Characteristics, Software Project Example. Goals are broad/qualitative (Improve customer experience), Objectives are specific/quantitative (Reduce complaint resolution time to <24 hours), Key Results are measurable outcomes (Achieve 90% satisfaction in Q1 survey). Shows hierarchical relationship with OKR framework application.

mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of objective setting, helping students see relationships between frameworks, processes, and practical considerations. The mind map serves as a complete study guide for exams.

mindMaps.centralConcept: Framework Comparison: SMART vs CLEAR vs OKR
mindMaps.purpose: Comparative mind map helping students understand when to apply each framework, particularly useful for exam questions on choosing appropriate objective-setting methodologies for different project types.
